question: What very recent evidence can improve reusable agent skills for software development in pi-package-goal?
basis: Retrieved metadata and abstracts only; no full-text screening or extraction acceptance was performed.

items.paper: SWE-Skills-Bench: Do Agent Skills Actually Help in Real-World Software Engineering?
items.conditions: 49 public skills, pinned GitHub repositories, about 565 paired task instances across six SWE subdomains, execution-based acceptance tests
items.visible_result: The abstract reports 39/49 skills with zero pass-rate improvement, +1.2% average gain, token overhead up to 451%, seven specialized skills with meaningful gains, and three regressions attributed to version-mismatched guidance.
items.bounded_implication: Require paired skill/no-skill task evaluation, deterministic acceptance tests, token accounting, and version compatibility checks before claiming a skill helps.
items.confidence: directly relevant but recent preprint; abstract-level evidence

items.paper: SkillMOO: Multi-Objective Optimization of Agent Skills for Software Engineering
items.conditions: 16 SkillsBench SE tasks; 38 analyzed skill edits; pass rate and inference cost optimized together
items.visible_result: The abstract reports that pruning and substitution dominated successful edits and reports cost reductions up to 31.7% over static bundles under its benchmark conditions.
items.bounded_implication: Treat skill quality as utility, cost, and harmful-guidance risk—not completeness or pass rate alone; try deletion before expansion.
items.confidence: directly relevant but small benchmark and recent preprint; abstract-level evidence

items.paper: More Skills, Worse Agents? Skill Shadowing Degrades Performance When Expanding Skill Libraries
items.conditions: Libraries scaled from a small helpful set to 202 skills; pass-rate loss decomposed into selection and context effects
items.visible_result: The abstract reports degradation up to 21% and attributes the primary bottleneck to wrong-skill selection rather than enlarged context.
items.bounded_implication: Add routing regression tests, anti-triggers, overlap review, and bounded bundles; do not assume a larger catalog is better.
items.confidence: directly relevant but recent preprint; abstract-level evidence

items.paper: Toward User Comprehension Supports for LLM Agent Skill Specifications
items.conditions: Rule-based coding of 878 cybersecurity SKILL markdown specifications plus a six-skill DNS/C2 subset
items.visible_result: The abstract reports examples in 19.0% and all four comprehension anchors in 2.3%: operational basis, output contract, boundary disclosure, and example capability demonstration.
items.bounded_implication: Keep the four-anchor requirement in write-a-skill and test it on non-trivial first-party skills.
items.confidence: large corpus but domain-specific and recent preprint; abstract-level evidence

items.paper: Supply-Chain Poisoning Attacks Against LLM Coding Agent Skill Ecosystems
items.conditions: 1,070 generated adversarial skills from 81 seeds, 15 MITRE ATT&CK categories, four frameworks, five models
items.visible_result: The abstract reports document-driven payload bypass rates of 11.6%–33.5%; static analysis caught most cases but 2.5% evaded both detection and alignment.
items.bounded_implication: Review documentation examples and referenced scripts as executable supply-chain content; provenance and package tests alone are insufficient.
items.confidence: directly relevant adversarial study but recent preprint; abstract-level evidence

items.paper: Agent Skills Enable a New Class of Realistic and Trivially Simple Prompt Injections
items.conditions: Malicious instructions hidden in long skill files and referenced scripts; approval carry-over tested in a coding-agent scenario
items.visible_result: The abstract reports exfiltration-oriented prompt injections and bypass of system-level guardrails through reused approval scope.
items.bounded_implication: Minimize standing skill text, inspect every reference/script, and never let a prior narrow approval silently authorize a broader action.
items.confidence: directly relevant security demonstration but preprint; abstract-level evidence

items.paper: AgentLens: Production-Assessed Trajectory Reviews for Coding Agent Evaluation
items.conditions: Interactive code-agent trajectories; formal verification where available plus written trajectory review and pairwise comparison
items.visible_result: The abstract argues that binary task success misses instruction following, tool use, verification, recovery, and communication behavior.
items.bounded_implication: Evaluate representative skill runs as trajectories, not only final test status.
items.confidence: very recent benchmark preprint; methodology signal, not independent consensus

items.paper: Skill Coverage: A Test Adequacy Metric for Agent Skills
items.conditions: Natural-language skill constraints mapped to trajectory-level exercise and adherence signals
items.visible_result: The abstract proposes skill coverage because task outcomes alone do not show which instructions were exercised or followed.
items.bounded_implication: For critical skills, map each red line and verification rule to at least one scenario that exercises it.
items.confidence: very recent preprint; promising evaluation concept requiring local validation
